# Defined Degradation Modes

**Flexibility: Expected (SHOULD)**

## The Principle

Engineering systems SHOULD define explicit degraded operating modes for
foreseeable failure scenarios. When a dependency fails, a resource is
exhausted, or load exceeds capacity, the system SHOULD transition to a
pre-designed degraded state rather than failing in an uncontrolled,
unpredictable manner.

## Concept Foundation

- **JOS-C23 (Failure Inevitability)** — Failure is not an edge case; it
  is a certainty. Every dependency will eventually be unavailable, every
  disk will fill, every network link will drop. Systems that lack defined
  degradation modes treat failure as a surprise and respond with crashes,
  hung requests, or data corruption. Systems that define degradation modes
  treat failure as a design input and respond with reduced but predictable
  service.

## Why This Matters

The difference between a minor incident and a major outage is often the
difference between controlled and uncontrolled failure. A payment service
that queues transactions when the database is slow degrades gracefully;
one that times out and drops transactions fails catastrophically. Defined
degradation modes give operators options: they can communicate accurate
status to users, triage recovery efforts, and maintain partial service
rather than losing everything. They also make failure behavior testable —
you can simulate a dependency outage and verify that the system enters
its designed degraded state.

## Tension and Tradeoffs

Designing degradation modes is engineering effort that may never be
exercised. Teams face the temptation to skip this work for dependencies
that "never go down." History consistently punishes this optimism.
However, the principle does not demand degradation modes for every
conceivable failure — only for foreseeable ones. A service with three
external dependencies should have a degradation plan for each; it need
not plan for all three failing simultaneously on a Tuesday during a
solar flare. The investment should match the probability and impact of
the failure scenario.

## 1970/2070 Note

In 1970, degradation meant switching to manual backup procedures when
the mainframe went down. In 2070, it may mean AI systems autonomously
reconfiguring service topologies to route around failures. The principle
endures because failures endure: no system achieves perfect availability,
so every system must decide in advance what "less than perfect" looks
like.

## Baseline+Overlay Note

This eng-domain principle extends JOS's universal governance. It operates
in addition to, not instead of, meta-layer principles.
